summary: Shapiro recounts a woodworking mistake where he tried to sand down a too-thick humidor part using 400-grit sandpaper instead of low-grit. Despite producing enormous quantities of sawdust that gave the illusion of progress, he spent an hour making almost no real headway. The high grit number means more cutting surfaces producing many tiny particles—volume of sawdust without meaningful material removal. It's a simple lesson about using the right tool for the job and not confusing the appearance of productivity with actual progress.
key_points: High-grit sandpaper produces lots of fine sawdust that creates an illusion of rapid progress
Visible output (sawdust) is not the same as meaningful progress (wood removal)
Using the wrong tool can waste enormous time while feeling productive
The right approach is to match your tools to the scale of the task
money_quotes: I was generating HUGE quantities of sawdust, so I figured I must be making progress.
Higher grit means more cutting surfaces means more particles of sawdust… they're just really small particles.
It looks like you're hogging off a ton of wood, but of course you're not making much of a dent at all.
I made a stupid mistake recently, so of course I am coming to you to share.
An hour (and a sore arm) later I realized my mistake.
key_insight: Visible busyness and output can be deeply misleading—generating huge quantities of sawdust doesn't mean you're actually removing wood, and the appearance of productivity is not the same as real progress.
